Notice Of Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court'S Order Denying Motion For Relief From Default And Default Judgment Pursuant To Ccp 473;
Off Calendar Dec-07-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Nicholas K. Jay, Esq. (415.788.7300; nkjaylaw@gmail.com) of Law Offices of Gilbert Y. Jay for Plaintiff.
Huechi Wong, Esq. (510.901.0010; hwonglegal@gmail.com) for Defendant video Zoom audio.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JONATHAN BORNSTEIN'S Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court'S Order Denying Motion For Relief From Default And Default Judgment Pursuant To CCP 473:

Defendant Jonathan Bornstein's motion for reconsideration of the court's September 22, 2021 order denying defendant's motion for relief from default pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 473 is denied. The motion was timely filed within 10 days after service by mail of the original order. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1013.) As set forth in the order, the motion for relief from default is untimely, and the Court lacks authority to grant relief from the six-month limit in section 473(b). Moreover, a motion to reconsider must be "based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1008(a).) "Facts of which the party seeking reconsideration was aware at the time of the original ruling are not new or different." (In re Marriage of Herr (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 1463, 1468; see also New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212; Garcia v. Hejmadi (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 674, 689-691.) The moving party shall state by affidavit "what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1008.) In addition, the moving party, via its affidavit, must demonstrate diligence and provide a satisfactory explanation for failing to offer the new evidence in the first instance. (Herr, 174 Cal.App.4th at 1468.) Defendant fails to make any such showing, but instead merely repeats the same assertions made in his original declaration.

The default therefore will not be set aside. In light of the Court's ruling on the accompanying motion to vacate void default judgment, however, the default judgment must be set aside on other grounds.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of And Motion To Vacate Void Default Judgment; **Court Reporter Fees Paid**
Off Calendar Dec-07-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Nicholas K. Jay, Esq. (415.788.7300; nkjaylaw@gmail.com) of Law Offices of Gilbert Y. Jay for Plaintiff.
Huechi Wong, Esq. (510.901.0010; hwonglegal@gmail.com) for Defendant video Zoom audio.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JONATHAN BORNSTEIN's Motion To Vacate Void Default Judgment:

Defendant's motion to vacate void default judgment is granted. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 580(a), "[t]he relief granted to the plaintiff, if there is no answer, cannot exceed that demanded in the complaint, in the statement required by Section 425.11, or in the statement provided for by Section 425.115"; see also Code Civ. Proc. sec. 585(a) [same].) "In all default judgments, the demand sets a ceiling on recovery." (Greenup v. Rodman (1986) 42 Cal.3d 822, 824.) "Because that ceiling is jurisdictional, a default judgment is void when the damages are in excess of the damages specified in the complaint or the statement of damages. A void judgment may be challenged at any time." (David S. Karton, A Law Corp. v. Dougherty (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 133, 150 [holding that default judgment was void on the face of the record because it granted relief that exceeded the demand in the then operative first amended complaint].)

Here, exactly as in Airs Aromatics, LLC v. CBL Data Recovery Technologies, Inc. (2018) 23 Cal.App.5th 1013, the February 19, 2021 default judgment, which is in the amount of $404,498.54 in compensatory damages, $722.75 in costs, and $1,213,495.62 in punitive damages, is void because the trial court awarded damages in excess of that demanded in the complaint, which merely sought damages "in an amount according to proof." (Id. at 1018-1019; accord, Stein v. York (2010) 181 Cal.App.4th 320, 327 ["Because the complaint here did not specify the amount of damages sought, it cannot support a default judgment in any amount."].) Plaintiff's argument that Defendant had notice of the amount sought through its prove-up papers is unavailing. "Section 580 requires formal notice of damages sought through the complaint and does not consider whether a defendant had actual or constructive notice." (Id. at 1019.) "Vacating the default judgment does not vacate the default itself. On remand, [plaintiff] may . . . amend its complaint to state the full amount of damages sought. If it pursues the latter option, the default will be vacated, entitling defendants to either attack the pleadings, or answer the amended complaint." (Id. at 1025 (citations and quotations omitted).)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
